ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai crullers (pa thong ko) with salted egg and snow milk topping. FOODON_03302051 (fried dough food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Doughnuts and similar fried dough products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Doughnuts and similar fried dough products) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Thai-style crullers with salted egg and condensed milk topping.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving consists of 4 crullers (approximately 200 g total) served with salted egg sauce and snow milk drizzle, as typically portioned at Thai-style snack stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Doughnuts, yeast-leavened, with jelly filling (NDB 18249) and salted duck egg composition (USDA SR Legacy) and condensed milk (USDA FoodData Central #01077), weighted by approximate recipe proportions for Thai-style crullers with salted egg and snow milk topping.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Thai crullers with salted egg and snow milk exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA databases. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA fried dough / cruller base (approx. 60% of dish weight, ~380 kcal/100g), (2) salted duck egg yolk sauce (approx. 25% of dish weight, ~200 kcal/100g, high fat and sodium), and (3) condensed milk drizzle (approx. 15% of dish weight, ~321 kcal/100g). Weighted composite yields approximately 330 kcal/100g. All component sources are USDA FoodData Central approved sources. Confidence is low due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white bread and refined wheat flour fried dough products — analogous estimate based on GI of fried dough / doughnuts (GI ~76) and condensed milk contribution; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai crullers (pa thong ko) with salted egg and snow milk. GI estimated at 72 based on analogous fried wheat dough products (GI range 68–76 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The salted egg and condensed milk toppings contribute fat and sugar which may moderately lower the effective GI of the composite dish; GI set conservatively at 72 (high category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 76.0) / 100 = 54.7, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 38.0 × 2 = 76.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted duck egg
hypertension.guidance: Each serving (4 pcs, ~200 g) provides approximately 960 mg sodium — nearly 42% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg — primarily from the salted duck egg component; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or avoid this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index refined wheat dough with added condensed milk sugar load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 72 and a glycemic load of ~54.7 per serving, this dish causes a rapid and substantial postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption and pair with low-GI foods if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat, cholesterol, sodium, and trans fat from deep-frying and salted egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of deep-fried dough, salted duck egg yolk (high in cholesterol and saturated fat), and condensed milk results in a dish high in saturated fat (~13 g per serving), cholesterol (~190 mg per serving), and sodium (~960 mg per serving), all of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; regular consumption is not recommended for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crullers with Salted Egg and Snow Milk (4 pieces) is a Thai-inspired snack dish consisting of four pieces of pa thong ko — traditional deep-fried Chinese-style crullers made from leavened wheat dough — served with a rich salted duck egg yolk sauce and a drizzle of snow milk, which is a sweetened condensed or evaporated milk mixture. The crullers are golden, crispy on the outside and soft and airy on the inside, with a mildly savoury flavour from the dough itself. The salted egg sauce adds a creamy, intensely savoury and umami-rich coating, while the snow milk drizzle provides a sweet, milky contrast. This combination of salty, sweet, and rich flavours is characteristic of modern Thai-fusion snack culture and has become popular in Singapore's food delivery market. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense (approximately 330 kcal per 100 g, or 660 kcal per 4-piece serving), high in refined carbohydrates, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium. The high glycemic index (~72) and large glycemic load (~54.7 per serving) make it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The high sodium content (~960 mg per serving) is a concern for those with hypertension. The saturated fat and cholesterol from the salted egg yolk and deep-frying process are relevant risk factors for cardiovascular disease. This dish is not suitable for vegans due to egg and dairy content, and is not certified halal due to the use of salted duck egg from non-halal-certified sources in many establishments.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Thai crullers with salted egg and snow milk exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA databases). All nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition of analogous USDA component entries (fried dough base, salted duck egg, condensed milk). GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on fried wheat dough products. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. All component data drawn from USDA FoodData Central (approved secondary global source). 2. vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for this fried dough and salted egg composite in the source data. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries used for estimation. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries used for estimation. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries used for estimation. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries used for estimation. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries used for estimation. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries used for estimation. 9. The input JSON incorrectly classified this food under food_group 'Beverages'; corrected to 'Snacks' as this is a fried dough snack dish. 10. The input cuisine tag 'Thai' is retained as the dish is a Thai-style pa thong ko preparation. 11. Halal status is marked false as salted duck egg sourcing and frying oil certification cannot be confirmed from available data. 12. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this specific dish is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 13. The food_group in the input was 'Beverages' which is clearly erroneous for a fried dough snack; corrected to 'Snacks'.
